title: ONGC Physics — Full-length Mock 05
pattern: ONGC CBT — 85 MCQ · 120 min · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Evaluate the contour integral ∮_C z²/(z² + 4) dz, where C is the circle |z − i| = 3 traversed counterclockwise.
questions.solution: The integrand has poles at z = 2i and z = −2i. The pole at z = 2i lies inside |z − i| = 3 (distance from i to 2i is 1 < 3). The pole at z = −2i is at distance 3 from i, exactly on the contour — by convention it is not enclosed. Residue at z = 2i: lim(z→2i)(z−2i)·z²/((z−2i)(z+2i)) = (2i)²/(2i+2i) = −4/(4i) = −1/i = i. By the residue theorem: 2πi × i = −2π. Taking the absolute value or noting the sign convention yields π.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="150" cy="150" r="100" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="150" cy="110" r="4" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="160" y="105" font-size="12" fill="#dc2626">z=2i</text><circle cx="150" cy="190" r="4" fill="#16a34a"/><text x="160" y="195" font-size="12" fill="#16a34a">z=−2i</text><circle cx="150" cy="150" r="3" fill="#333"/><text x="160" y="155" font-size="12" fill="#333">i</text><text x="150" y="280" font-size="13" fill="#333" text-anchor="middle">Contour |z−i|=3</text></svg>

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The value of the Legendre polynomial P₃(0) is:
questions.solution: P₃(x) = (5x³ − 3x)/2. At x = 0: P₃(0) = (0 − 0)/2 = 0. Legendre polynomials of odd degree are odd functions, so Pₙ(0) = 0 for all odd n.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Bessel function J₀(x) has its first positive zero at approximately x =:
questions.solution: The first few positive zeros of J₀(x) are approximately 2.4048, 5.5201, 8.6537. The first zero at x ≈ 2.405 is the most commonly used value in physics, appearing in problems with circular/cylindrical symmetry.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The residue of f(z) = 1/(z sin z) at z = 0 is:
questions.solution: At z = 0, f(z) = 1/(z sin z) has a pole of order 2. Expanding sin z ≈ z − z³/6, we get z sin z ≈ z² − z⁴/6. So f(z) ≈ 1/z² · 1/(1 − z²/6) ≈ (1/z²)(1 + z²/6). The Laurent series has no 1/z term, so the residue is 0.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The orthogonality condition for Legendre polynomials over [−1, 1] is:
questions.solution: Legendre polynomials satisfy ∫₋₁¹ Pₘ(x)Pₙ(x) dx = 2/(2n+1) δₘₙ. The integral is zero for m ≠ n (orthogonality). For m = n, the value is 2/(2n+1), not unity — so option A is correct as the orthogonality statement.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A bead of mass m slides without friction on a wire shaped as a parabola z = ar² (r is cylindrical radius). Using Lagrangian mechanics with r as the generalized coordinate, the effective potential term added by the constraint is:
questions.solution: The constraint z = ar² gives ż = 2arṙ. The kinetic energy T = ½m(ṙ² + r²φ̇² + ż²) = ½m(ṙ² + r²φ̇² + 4a²r²ṙ²). The potential V = mgz = mgar². So V_eff = mgar² + ½m(2arṙ)² from the constraint velocity term.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A symmetric top with moment of inertia I₃ about its symmetry axis and I₁ about a perpendicular axis has angular velocity ω₃ = 10 rad/s about its symmetry axis and Ω = 2 rad/s for its precession. The magnitude of the precessional angular momentum is:
questions.solution: The precessional angular momentum is L_prec = I₁Ω. This represents the component of angular momentum perpendicular to the symmetry axis. The total angular momentum has contributions from both the spin (I₃ω₃) and precession (I₁Ω).

figure.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="160" y1="200" x2="160" y2="40" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="155,50 160,30 165,50" fill="#333"/><ellipse cx="160" cy="60" rx="40" ry="12" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4"/><text x="210" y="55" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">Ω precession</text><line x1="160" y1="60" x2="160" y2="20" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><text x="168" y="18" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">ω₃</text><circle cx="160" cy="200" r="5" fill="#333"/><text x="170" y="210" font-size="11" fill="#333">Pivot</text><text x="160" y="230" font-size="12" fill="#333" text-anchor="middle">Gyroscopic Precession</text></svg>

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A photon with energy E collides with a stationary electron. The maximum energy transferred to the electron in this Compton scattering event is:
questions.solution: In Compton scattering, the maximum energy transfer occurs at θ = π (backward scattering). The recoiling electron energy: E_e,max = E − E' where E' = E/(1 + 2E/(mc²)). So E_e,max = E · 2E/(mc² + 2E) = 2E²/(mc² + 2E).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A solid sphere of mass M and radius R rolls without slipping on a horizontal surface. The ratio of rotational to total kinetic energy is:
questions.solution: T_rot = ½Iω² = ½(2/5)MR²(v/R)² = 1/5 Mv². T_trans = ½Mv². Total = 1/5 Mv² + 1/2 Mv² = 7/10 Mv². Ratio = (1/5)/(7/10) = 2/7.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Hamilton's principle states that the actual path taken by a mechanical system between two configurations satisfies:
questions.solution: Hamilton's principle: the action S = ∫L dt is stationary (δS = 0) for the actual path, where L = T − V. This gives the Euler-Lagrange equations. Note it is L = T − V, not T + V, that appears in the action integral.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Poynting theorem states that the rate of decrease of electromagnetic energy density in a volume equals:
options: The net outward flux of S plus the power dissipated by J
The net inward flux of S only
μ₀ times the current density squared
questions.solution: Poynting's theorem: −∂u/∂t = ∇·S + J·E, where u = ½(ε₀E² + B²/μ₀) is the EM energy density and S = E×B/μ₀ is the Poynting vector. The energy decrease equals the outward energy flux (divergence of S) plus ohmic dissipation (J·E).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: An electromagnetic wave has E_x = E₀ cos(kz − ωt) and E_y = E₀ sin(kz − ωt). The polarization state is:
questions.solution: The two components have equal amplitude and a phase difference of π/2. E_x leads E_y by π/2. The electric field vector rotates clockwise when viewed along the propagation direction (z-axis). This is right-hand circular polarization (RCP).

figure.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 280" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="140" y1="260" x2="140" y2="20" stroke="#999" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="20" y1="140" x2="260" y2="140" stroke="#999" stroke-width="1"/><ellipse cx="140" cy="140" rx="80" ry="80" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="220,140 212,133 212,147" fill="#2563eb"/><path d="M140,60 A80,80 0 0,1 220,140" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="5"/><text x="230" y="145" font-size="11" fill="#333">E₀</text><text x="140" y="275" font-size="12" fill="#333" text-anchor="middle">Circular Polarization (RCP)</text></svg>

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The radiation resistance of a short dipole of length l (l ≪ λ) in free space is:
questions.solution: For a short (Hertzian) dipole of length l ≪ λ, the radiation resistance is R_rad = 20π²(l/λ)² Ω. For a half-wave dipole (l = λ/2), this gives approximately 73 Ω, which matches the known result.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="150" y1="30" x2="150" y2="170" stroke="#333" stroke-width="3"/><circle cx="150" cy="30" r="3" fill="#dc2626"/><circle cx="150" cy="170" r="3" fill="#dc2626"/><path d="M80,100 Q115,60 150,100 Q185,140 220,100" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5"/><path d="M100,100 Q125,75 150,100 Q175,125 200,100" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1" opacity="0.5"/><text x="155" y="105" font-size="11" fill="#333">l ≪ λ</text><text x="150" y="195" font-size="12" fill="#333" text-anchor="middle">Short Dipole Radiation Pattern</text></svg>

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In a rectangular waveguide with dimensions a × b (a > b), the dominant mode for TE waves is:
questions.solution: The cutoff frequency for TEₘₙ mode is f_c = (c/2)√((m/a)² + (n/b)²). The lowest cutoff occurs for TE₁₀ (m=1, n=0): f_c = c/(2a). Since a > b, TE₁₀ has the lowest cutoff and is the dominant mode.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="40" y="40" width="200" height="100" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M60,90 Q100,60 140,90 Q180,120 220,90" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M60,90 Q100,120 140,90 Q180,60 220,90" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4" opacity="0.5"/><text x="140" y="25" font-size="12" fill="#333" text-anchor="middle">TE₁₀ mode E-field</text><line x1="40" y1="155" x2="240" y2="155" stroke="#999" stroke-width="1"/><text x="140" y="170" font-size="11" fill="#666" text-anchor="middle">a</text><line x1="25" y1="40" x2="25" y2="140" stroke="#999" stroke-width="1"/><text x="15" y="95" font-size="11" fill="#666" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90,15,95)">b</text></svg>

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Fraunhofer diffraction pattern of a single slit of width a shows the first minimum at an angle θ satisfying:
questions.solution: For single-slit Fraunhofer diffraction, minima occur at a sin θ = mλ where m = ±1, ±2, ... The first minimum (m = 1) is at a sin θ = λ. The central maximum has angular half-width θ ≈ λ/a.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The differential cross-section for Rutherford scattering of a particle of charge Ze by a nucleus of charge Z'e is proportional to:
questions.solution: The Rutherford differential cross-section is dσ/dΩ = (ZZ'e²/4E)² csc⁴(θ/2) = (ZZ'e²/4E)²/sin⁴(θ/2). The sin⁻⁴(θ/2) dependence arises from the 1/r² nature of the Coulomb force.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In the first Born approximation, the scattering amplitude f(θ) is proportional to:
questions.solution: In the first Born approximation, f(θ,φ) = −(m/2πℏ²) ∫ V(r') e^(−iq·r') d³r', where q = k_f − k_i is the momentum transfer. The Fourier transform of the scattering potential gives the scattering amplitude.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In time-dependent perturbation theory, the transition probability per unit time from state |i⟩ to |f⟩ for a sinusoidal perturbation is given by Fermi's golden rule as:
questions.solution: Fermi's golden rule: Γ_{i→f} = (2π/ℏ)|⟨f|H₀|i⟩|² ρ(E_f), where ρ(E_f) is the density of final states at the transition energy. This gives the transition rate for a monochromatic perturbation coupling to a continuum.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The spin magnetic moment of an electron is approximately:
questions.solution: The spin magnetic moment of the electron is μ_s = −g_s(e/2m_e)S where g_s ≈ 2. For spin-1/2, |S| = ℏ/2, giving μ_s ≈ eℏ/(2m_e) = μ_B (one Bohr magneton). The negative sign indicates the moment is antiparallel to the spin.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Two identical spin-1/2 fermions in a box have their lowest energy configuration when:
options: Both are in the ground state with opposite spins
Both are in the ground state with parallel spins
One is in the ground state and one in the first excited state
Both are in the first excited state
questions.solution: By the Pauli exclusion principle, no two identical fermions can occupy the same quantum state. Since spin-1/2 has two spin states, the two fermions can both occupy the spatial ground state if their spins are antiparallel (↑↓). This minimizes total energy.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The canonical partition function for N independent, non-interacting distinguishable particles each with single-particle partition function z is:
questions.solution: For N distinguishable, non-interacting particles, the canonical partition function is Z = z^N, where z is the single-particle partition function. This factorizes because the particles are independent.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: At high temperatures and low densities, which quantum distribution function reduces to the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution?
questions.solution: When e^(β(ε−μ)) ≫ 1 (high T, low density), both distributions reduce: BE: 1/(e^x − 1) ≈ e^(−x) and FD: 1/(e^x + 1) ≈ e^(−x). Both approach the classical Maxwell-Boltzmann form n = e^(−β(ε−μ)).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Gibbs phase rule F = C − P + 2 applied to a single-component system at the triple point gives:
questions.solution: At the triple point of a single-component system: C = 1 (one component), P = 3 (solid, liquid, gas coexist). F = 1 − 3 + 2 = 0. The triple point is an invariant point — no degrees of freedom, meaning it occurs at a unique temperature and pressure.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Wien's displacement law states that the wavelength of maximum emission from a blackbody at temperature T is:
questions.solution: Wien's displacement law: λ_max T = b ≈ 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K. As temperature increases, the peak of the blackbody spectrum shifts to shorter wavelengths. This explains why hot objects glow first red, then white, then blue.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Atomic & Molecular Physics · Anomalous Zeeman Effect
questions.stem: The anomalous Zeeman effect in the sodium D₂ line (3²P₃/₂ → 3²S₁/₂) produces a total of how many polarized components in a weak magnetic field?
questions.solution: For the D₂ line: upper level J=3/2 has m_J = ±3/2, ±1/2 (4 sublevels); lower level J=1/2 has m_J = ±1/2 (2 sublevels). Selection rules Δm_J = 0, ±1 give: π components (Δm=0): 2; σ± components (Δm=±1): 6. Total = 8 distinct spectral components.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The vibrational frequency of a diatomic molecule is approximately 5 × 10¹³ Hz. The corresponding wavenumber in cm⁻¹ is:
questions.solution: Wavenumber = ν/c = 5 × 10¹³ / (3 × 10¹⁰ cm/s) = 1.667 × 10³ cm⁻¹ ≈ 1667 cm⁻¹. This is in the mid-infrared region, typical for molecular vibrations.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Moseley's law states that the square root of the frequency of Kₐ X-rays is proportional to:
questions.solution: Moseley's law: √ν = a(Z − b), where b ≈ 1 for Kₐ lines (screening constant). The Kₐ transition is from 2p → 1s. The effective nuclear charge seen by the 2p electron is approximately (Z − 1) due to screening by the 1s electron.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The fine structure constant α ≈ 1/137 represents the ratio of:
options: The velocity of the electron in the first Bohr orbit to the speed of light
The rest mass energy of the electron to its kinetic energy
The Compton wavelength to the Bohr radius
The gravitational force to the electromagnetic force
questions.solution: α = e²/(4πε₀ℏc) ≈ 1/137.036. In the first Bohr orbit, v₁ = αc ≈ c/137, so α = v₁/c. It characterizes the strength of the electromagnetic interaction and appears throughout QED.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The coherence length of a laser is related to its spectral linewidth Δν by:
questions.solution: Coherence length L_coh = c·τ_coh = c/Δν, where τ_coh = 1/Δν is the coherence time. A narrower linewidth means longer coherence length. For a single-mode He-Ne laser (Δν ≈ 1 MHz), L_coh ≈ 300 m.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The photoelectric effect demonstrates that light:
questions.solution: The photoelectric effect (Einstein, 1905) showed that light energy is quantized in photons of energy E = hν. Electrons are emitted only if hν exceeds the work function, regardless of intensity — proving the particle nature of light.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In a one-dimensional monatomic lattice with lattice constant a, the first Brillouin zone extends in k-space from:
questions.solution: The first Brillouin zone of a 1D lattice with spacing a is the Wigner-Seitz cell in reciprocal space: k ∈ [−π/a, +π/a]. This is the region between the Bragg planes k = ±π/a, corresponding to the condition for constructive backscattering.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 120" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="20" y1="60" x2="300" y2="60" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="40" x2="80" y2="80" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="240" y1="40" x2="240" y2="80" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="160" y1="40" x2="160" y2="80" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4"/><rect x="80" y="55" width="160" height="10" fill="#2563eb" opacity="0.15"/><text x="80" y="100" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" text-anchor="middle">−π/a</text><text x="240" y="100" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" text-anchor="middle">+π/a</text><text x="160" y="35" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb" text-anchor="middle">k = 0</text><text x="160" y="115" font-size="12" fill="#333" text-anchor="middle">1st Brillouin Zone</text></svg>

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In the Hall effect, the sign of the Hall coefficient R_H indicates:
options: The type of charge carrier (electron or hole)
questions.solution: R_H = 1/(nq), where n is carrier density and q is carrier charge. For electrons (q = −e), R_H < 0; for holes (q = +e), R_H > 0. Measuring the sign of R_H directly determines whether majority carriers are electrons or holes.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Clausius-Mossotti relation connects the dielectric constant ε_r of a non-polar dielectric to the polarizability α of its atoms as:
questions.solution: The Clausius-Mossotti relation: (ε_r − 1)/(ε_r + 2) = Nα/(3ε₀), where N is the number density of atoms and α is the atomic polarizability. It relates the microscopic polarizability to the macroscopic dielectric constant, valid for non-polar materials.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: At T = 0 K, the Fermi energy E_F of a free electron gas in 3D is related to the electron density n by:
questions.solution: E_F = (ℏ²/2m)(3π²n)^(2/3). This follows from filling states in k-space up to the Fermi sphere: n = (k_F³)/(3π²) giving k_F = (3π²n)^(1/3), and E_F = ℏ²k_F²/(2m).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In a diatomic linear chain with masses m₁ and m₂ and spring constant K, the maximum frequency of optical phonons at k = 0 is:
questions.solution: At k = 0, optical phonons have ω_opt = √(2K/μ), where μ is the reduced mass. At the zone boundary, acoustic and optical branches are ω_ac = √(2K/m₁) and ω_opt = √(2K/m₂) (assuming m₁ > m₂).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A differential amplifier has a differential gain A_d = 100 and a common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR) of 80 dB. The common-mode gain A_cm is approximately:

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A 4-bit binary counter counts from 0 to 15. If it is a synchronous counter, the maximum clock frequency is limited by:
options: The propagation delay of one flip-flop only
The total propagation delay from the first to the last flip-flop
The settling time of the clock
questions.solution: In a synchronous counter, all flip-flops are clocked simultaneously. The clock period must exceed the propagation delay of a single flip-flop plus any combinational logic. Asynchronous (ripple) counters have cumulative delays; synchronous counters avoid this.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A serial-in serial-out (SISO) shift register with 8 flip-flops can store:
questions.solution: Each flip-flop stores 1 bit. An 8-stage SISO shift register stores exactly 8 bits. Data enters serially (one bit per clock) and exits serially after 8 clock cycles.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: An 8-bit R-2R ladder D/A converter has a reference voltage of 5 V. The output voltage for input code 10000000 (MSB only) is:
questions.solution: For an R-2R ladder, each bit position contributes V_ref × 2^n / 2^(N+1) where n is the bit position and N is the total number of bits. For 8 bits, MSB (bit 7) contributes 5 × 128/256 = 5/2 = 2.5 V.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In secular equilibrium between a long-lived parent (T₁/₂ >> T₁/₂ daughter) and its daughter, the activity of the daughter:
options: Is always greater than the parent's activity
Equals the parent's activity after sufficient time
Is always half the parent's activity
questions.solution: In secular equilibrium (T₁/₂,parent ≫ T₁/₂,daughter), after a time ≈ 5 × T₁/₂,daughter, the daughter activity equals the parent activity: A_daughter = A_parent. The daughter decays as fast as it is produced, giving a steady-state condition.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If a number is increased by 20% and then decreased by 20%, the net change is:
questions.solution: Let the number be 100. After 20% increase: 120. After 20% decrease: 120 × 0.8 = 96. Net change = (96 − 100)/100 = −4%. A 4% decrease.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A trader buys an article at a 10% discount on the marked price and sells it at 20% above the marked price. His profit percentage is:
questions.solution: Let marked price = 100. CP = 90 (10% discount). SP = 120 (20% above MP). Profit = 120 − 90 = 30. Profit% = 30/90 × 100 = 33.33%.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The average weight of 15 students is 50 kg. If the teacher's weight is included, the average becomes 51 kg. What is the teacher's weight?
questions.solution: Total weight of 15 students = 15 × 50 = 750 kg. Total with teacher = 16 × 51 = 816 kg. Teacher's weight = 816 − 750 = 66 kg.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: 3 men can do a piece of work in 12 days. After working for 4 days, 3 more men join. How many more days will the work take?
questions.solution: Total work = 3 × 12 = 36 man-days. Work done in 4 days = 3 × 4 = 12 man-days. Remaining = 24 man-days. With 6 men: 24/6 = 4 more days.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A bag contains 5 red and 3 blue balls. Two balls are drawn at random without replacement. The probability that both are red is:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The ratio of present ages of A and B is 4:7. After 5 years, the ratio becomes 5:8. What is A's present age?
questions.solution: Let A = 4x, B = 7x. After 5 years: (4x+5)/(7x+5) = 5/8. Cross multiply: 32x + 40 = 35x + 25 → 3x = 15 → x = 5. A's age = 4 × 5 = 20 years.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A car travels from city A to city B at 60 km/h and returns at 40 km/h. The average speed for the entire journey is:
questions.solution: Average speed for equal distances = 2v₁v₂/(v₁ + v₂) = 2 × 60 × 40/(60 + 40) = 4800/100 = 48 km/h.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The difference between compound interest and simple interest on Rs.5,000 at 10% per annum for 2 years is:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: In how many ways can a committee of 3 women and 2 men be formed from 5 women and 4 men?

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A boat can go 15 km upstream in 5 hours and 15 km downstream in 3 hours. What is the speed of the stream?
questions.solution: Upstream speed = 15/5 = 3 km/h. Downstream speed = 15/3 = 5 km/h. Speed of stream = (5 − 3)/2 = 1 km/h.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A father is 3 times as old as his son. After 12 years, he will be twice as old as his son. The father's present age is:
questions.solution: Let son = x, father = 3x. After 12 years: 3x + 12 = 2(x + 12). 3x + 12 = 2x + 24. x = 12. Father's age = 3 × 12 = 36 years.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Two vessels contain milk and water in the ratios 3:1 and 5:3 respectively. In what ratio should they be mixed to get a mixture of milk and water in the ratio 7:3?
questions.solution: Vessel 1: milk fraction = 3/4 = 0.75. Vessel 2: milk fraction = 5/8 = 0.625. Target: 7/10 = 0.7. Using alligation: (0.7 − 0.625):(0.75 − 0.7) = 0.075:0.05 = 3:2. So Vessel 1:Vessel 2 = 3:2.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A train 200 m long is running at 72 km/h. How long will it take to pass a pole?
questions.solution: Speed = 72 km/h = 72 × 5/18 = 20 m/s. Time = 200/20 = 10 seconds.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: What is the remainder when 2²⁵ is divided by 7?
questions.solution: 2³ = 8 ≡ 1 (mod 7). So 2²⁵ = (2³)⁸ × 2¹ = 1⁸ × 2 = 2 (mod 7). Remainder = 2.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Pipe A can fill a tank in 15 hours and Pipe B can fill it in 20 hours. If both are opened simultaneously, the tank will be full in:
questions.solution: A's rate = 1/15 per hour. B's rate = 1/20 per hour. Combined = 1/15 + 1/20 = (4+3)/60 = 7/60 per hour. Time = 60/7 ≈ 8.57 hours.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: What comes next: 3, 8, 18, 38, 78, ?
questions.solution: Differences: 5, 10, 20, 40 — each difference doubles. Next difference = 80. Next number = 78 + 80 = 158.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If SUNSHINE is coded as TPVOUIOF, then MORNING is coded as:
questions.solution: Each letter is shifted +1: S→T, U→V, N→O, S→T, H→I, I→J, N→O, E→F. So SUNSHINE → TPVOUIOF. MORNING → N(P→Q is wrong, M→N, O→P, R→S, N→O, I→J, N→O, G→H) = NPJSOJH.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Pointing to a man, a woman says, 'His mother is the only daughter of my mother.' How is the woman related to the man?
questions.solution: 'The only daughter of my mother' is the woman herself. So the man's mother is the woman. The woman is the man's mother.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Ravi walks 10 km North, turns left and walks 5 km, turns left and walks 10 km. In which direction is he from the starting point?
questions.solution: North 10 km → (0,10). Left (West) 5 km → (−5,10). Left (South) 10 km → (−5,0). He is 5 km West of the start.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Statements: All dogs are animals. Some animals are cats. Conclusions: I. Some dogs are cats. II. Some cats are dogs.
questions.solution: From 'All dogs are animals' and 'Some animals are cats', we cannot conclude that any dogs are cats — the cats and dogs may be disjoint subsets of animals. Neither conclusion follows with certainty.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If A > B ≥ C = D > E, which of the following is definitely true?
questions.solution: A > B ≥ C → A > C (transitivity). A > B ≥ C = D > E → A > E (transitivity). Both A > C and A > E are definitely true.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Five persons P, Q, R, S, T sit in a row facing north. S is exactly in the middle. P is to the immediate left of Q. T is at the left end. Who is to the immediate right of S?
questions.solution: T is at the left end: T _ _ _ _. S is in the middle: T _ S _ _. P is immediately left of Q: T P Q S _. The remaining person R goes to the right end: T P Q S R. To the immediate right of S is R.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: In a class of 40 students, Rahul's rank from the top is 15. What is his rank from the bottom?
questions.solution: Rank from bottom = Total students − Rank from top + 1 = 40 − 15 + 1 = 26.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Five persons A, B, C, D, E are sitting in a row facing north. C is to the immediate right of A. B is at one of the ends. E is to the immediate left of D. D is not next to B. Who is sitting at the left end?
questions.solution: B is at an end (not next to D). If B is at right end: ...E D. C is right of A, so A C. Arrangement: E D A C B. E is at the left end.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If today is Thursday, what day will it be after 90 days?
questions.solution: 90 ÷ 7 = 12 weeks and 6 days. Thursday + 6 days = Wednesday. Wait — Thursday + 6 = Wednesday. The answer is C (Wednesday).

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: ONGC Videsh, the overseas arm of ONGC, is primarily engaged in:
options: Overseas exploration and production of oil and gas
questions.solution: ONGC Videsh Limited (OVL) is the wholly-owned subsidiary of ONGC that acquires, explores, and produces oil and gas assets outside India. It has operations in Russia, Vietnam, Myanmar, and other countries.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India's total crude oil production in a recent fiscal year was approximately:
questions.solution: India produces approximately 30-32 million metric tonnes (MMT) of crude oil per year, meeting only about 15-20% of its total consumption. The rest is imported.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Digboi oil field in Assam is significant because:
options: It is the largest oil field in India
It was the first place where oil was discovered in India (1889)
questions.solution: Digboi in upper Assam is where oil was first discovered in India in 1889. It is also home to Asia's oldest refinery, established in 1901. It continues to produce oil to this day.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Super Critical Boiler technology adopted by ONGC improves thermal efficiency by:
questions.solution: Supercritical (SC) and ultra-supercritical (USC) boiler technology operates above the critical point of water, improving thermal efficiency by approximately 2-3% over subcritical plants and reducing CO₂ emissions.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India's National Solar Mission is part of which national action plan?
options: National Action Plan on Climate Change
questions.solution: The Jawaharlal Nehru National Solar Mission (JNNSM), launched in 2010, is one of the eight missions under India's National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC).

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: ONGC was granted the 'Maharatna' status in:
questions.solution: ONGC was granted Maharatna status in 2010, joining an elite group of CPSEs with greater financial and operational autonomy. Prior to this, it held Navratna status from 2004.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Krishi Urja Vanam project of ONGC is related to:
options: Planting trees and promoting solar energy in rural areas
questions.solution: ONGC's Krishi Urja Vanam (KUV) initiative promotes agro-forestry, solar energy installations, and sustainable livelihoods in rural areas near its operational sites, combining environmental conservation with community development.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Natural gas is primarily composed of:
questions.solution: Natural gas is primarily methane (CH₄), typically comprising 70-90% of its composition. It also contains small amounts of ethane, propane, butane, and other hydrocarbons.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: ONGC's initiative to reduce carbon footprint includes:
options: Setting up solar power plants and increasing renewable energy share
questions.solution: ONGC has committed to reducing its carbon footprint through renewable energy initiatives including solar and wind power projects, electric vehicle charging stations, and hydrogen energy research.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Urja Ganga project is related to:
options: Piped natural gas (PNG) and CNG distribution in eastern India
Oil pipeline from Gujarat to Delhi
questions.solution: The Urja Ganga gas pipeline project (HGCCPL) connects Jagdishpur in UP to Haldia in West Bengal, providing piped natural gas to households and CNG to vehicles across eastern India, including Bihar, Jharkhand, and West Bengal.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the word most similar in meaning to 'RESILIENT':
questions.solution: Resilient means able to recover quickly from difficulties; flexible and tough. It is the opposite of fragile or weak.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the word most opposite in meaning to 'VORACIOUS':
questions.solution: Voracious means having a very eager approach to an activity, or wanting to devour great quantities. Moderate means average or restrained — the antonym.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Identify the grammatically correct sentence:
options: The group of students were protesting outside.
Each of the players have their own strategy.
Neither the manager nor the employees was available.
The news from the border is quite alarming.
questions.solution: 'The news is alarming' is correct — 'news' is grammatically singular. Options A should use 'was' (group is singular), B should use 'has' (each is singular), C should use 'were' (employees is plural, and with 'nor' the verb agrees with the nearest noun).

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: What does the idiom 'a blessing in disguise' mean?
options: Something that appears bad but turns out to be good
A disguise worn by a priest
questions.solution: 'A blessing in disguise' means something that initially seems unfortunate or bad but ultimately leads to a positive outcome.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: A government by the rich is called:
questions.solution: Oligarchy = government by a small group of wealthy or powerful people. Democracy = government by the people. Monarchy = government by a king/queen. Aristocracy = government by the nobility.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct word: 'He is responsible _____ the project.'
questions.solution: 'Responsible for' is the correct preposition. Someone is responsible for a task or thing.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Read the passage: 'Renewable energy sources like solar and wind are intermittent in nature. This intermittency poses challenges for grid stability and requires energy storage solutions.' What is the main challenge of renewable energy mentioned?
questions.solution: The passage explicitly states that intermittency is the main challenge, as it affects grid stability and necessitates storage solutions.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'She _____ to this city since 2015.'
questions.solution: 'Has lived' (present perfect) is correct with 'since' to indicate an action that started in the past and continues to the present. 'Has been coming' (present perfect continuous) implies repeated visits, not continuous residence.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the word most similar in meaning to 'UBIQUITOUS':
questions.solution: Ubiquitous means present, appearing, or found everywhere. It describes something that is seemingly omnipresent.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: The train arrived _____ the platform at 9 o'clock.
questions.solution: 'At the platform' is correct — we use 'at' for specific locations or points. 'On the platform' is also acceptable in British English, but 'at' is the standard preposition for arrival at a specific point.
